activity_metadata.objective: Students will compare and contrast the motivations of individuals across three texts in a short written response.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the Write segment of the "Margaret Bourke-White: Fearless Photographer" Close Read. This is the second of two lessons for this text. This lesson focuses on comparative writing across "Donna O'Meara: The Volcano Lady," "Dare to be Creative!," and "Margaret Bourke-White: Fearless Photographer."
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity supports the end-of-unit assessment by developing students' ability to compare and contrast ideas across informational texts. Students practice using textual evidence to support analysis and develop comparative analytical writing skills by examining how three different authors present similar themes about motivation and adventure. The focus on identifying what authors convey through their narrative choices prepares students for assessment questions that require them to analyze how central ideas are developed through supporting details across multiple texts.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This activity connects to the unit's essential question "How do we know what to do when there are no instructions?" by examining how Donna O'Meara, Madeleine L'Engle, and Margaret Bourke-White each navigated situations without clear guidance. O'Meara had no roadmap for becoming a volcano researcher at age 32. L'Engle embraces uncertainty in her writing, letting her books "shake her universe." Bourke-White entered a field with no precedent for women, guided only by her parents' motto "You can." All three demonstrate that when there are no instructions, individuals must draw on inner passion, curiosity, and determination to create their own paths forward.
activity_metadata.text_title:
Donna O’Meara: The Volcano Lady
Dare to be Creative!
Margaret Bourke-White: Fearless Photographer
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Paired Reading: Comparing Motivations

Excerpt 1: Donna O'Meara: The Volcano Lady

(1) Frightening experiences on top of a volcano are not unusual for Donna O'Meara. For over 25 years, she has worked with Steve to photograph and study volcanoes all over the world. They hope their documentation will someday be a written and visual record of information that helps scientists to better predict volcanic eruptions.

(2) O'Meara grew up in the New England countryside. There are no volcanoes in Connecticut, but in the spring and summer there were fierce thunder and lightning storms that thrilled Donna. In school, her favorite classes were earth science and biology. However, instead of turning her love for science into a career after graduation, she became an artist, photographer, and writer. As she worked on different magazines and books, she gradually began to realize that something was missing in her life.

(3) When Donna went back to school at the age of 32 to study science, her passion for volcanoes began. She took geology classes to learn more about what rocks and soil tell us about the earth. She found out that volcanism is one of the most dynamic forces in nature. Volcanoes constantly shape and change the earth. Many islands, such as the islands that make up Hawaii, were formed by volcanic activity.

(4) In 1986, Donna visited her first volcano as Steve's research assistant. After dodging lava bombs and feeling the heat from underground lava melting her shoes, Donna was hooked. The following year, she and Steve were married on lava that had oozed from Kilauea on Hawaii and hardened. Lava that hardens creates new landforms, and some volcanoes, such as Surtsey off the coast of Iceland, actually create new islands!


Excerpt 2: Dare to be Creative!

(5) Russell suggests that people are zealous when they are not completely certain they are right. I agree with him. When I find myself hotly defending something, when I am, in fact, zealous, it is time for me to step back and examine whatever it is that has me so hot under the collar. Do I think it's going to threaten my comfortable rut? Make me change and grow?—and growing always causes growing pains. Am I afraid to ask questions?

(6) Sometimes. But I believe that good questions are more important than answers, and the best children's books ask questions, and make the reader ask questions. And every new question is going to disturb someone's universe.

(7) Writing fiction is definitely a universe disturber, and for the writer, first of all. My books push me and prod me and make me ask questions I might otherwise avoid. I start a book, having lived with the characters for several years, during the writing of other books, and I have a pretty good idea of where the story is going and what I hope it's going to say. And then, once I get deep into the writing, unexpected things begin to happen, things which make me question, and which sometimes really shake my universe.


Excerpt 3: Margaret Bourke-White: Fearless Photographer

(8) Joseph White and Minnie Bourke, however, never told their daughter what to think and dream about. Instead, young Margaret, or "Peg" as her friends called her, got plenty of attention and encouragement from her parents to explore her world. Early on, they taught her to work hard and to go after what she wanted. They even gave her a motto: "You can." It's no wonder Margaret Bourke-White grew up to be one of the most accomplished women and talked-about photographers of the twentieth century.

(9) Many photographers today owe thanks to Margaret. From the time she started taking photographs and recognized that they could stir up feelings to the culmination of her long career as a photojournalist, Margaret was a trailblazer. She shaped the art of photography and the profession of photojournalism and showed that women photographers could travel all over the world and work alongside men in dangerous situations.

(10) During the 1930s and 1940s, Margaret's adventurous attitude and perseverance paved the way for women to take on roles beyond the norm. Rather than snapping photos of high-society parties as other female photographers had done before her, she marched into steel plants and combat zones. She proved to women that they had every right to pursue the careers they wanted.

(11) Through her work, Margaret became a role model for working women as well as a strong voice for the poor and powerless. She earned the respect of powerful businessmen when women were discouraged from working. When she died in 1971, she left behind not only an amazing photographic record of the human experience, she also left a message for women all over the world who wanted to make an impact: "You can."

responses.bucket_rationale: The response covers all three individuals and attempts to compare their motivations, but it is vague and somewhat inaccurate. The claim that all are motivated by 'love and compassion for attention grabbing' is not fully supported by the texts. Donna is motivated by a passion for science and helping predict eruptions, Margaret by breaking barriers and giving a voice to the powerless, and L'Engle by creative questioning and personal growth. The response also repeats earlier points without adding new, specific details about motivations. There are at least two quality issues: vagueness and partial inaccuracy, so 'partial-understanding' is the best fit.

USER_15.question:
What motivates each individual in these three texts, and how are their motivations similar or different? 


Respond to this question in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, at least three pieces of evidence (one from each text), and an explanation of how your evidence supports your claim

responses.content: All three individuals are motivated by passion—Donna O'Meara is passionate about volcanoes and wants to help scientists predict eruptions, the author of "Dare to be Creative" is motivated by creative questioning and personal growth through writing, and Margaret Bourke-White is driven by encouragement from her parents, a desire to break barriers for women, and to give a voice to the powerless. Their motivations are similar because they all follow their passions, but they are different in what they hope to achieve with their work.

Donna O Meara is passionate about volcanoes because of the fierce thunder and lighting storms that we took classes on earth science and biology.
Margaret Bourke White was driven by encouragement so much by her parents that she became a photographer and took photos in steel plants and combat zones just to break barriers for women and to give a voice to the powerless.
the author of Dare to be Creative is motivated by creating questions for those who are a afraid to ask the questions because of being afraid of a wrong answer.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies motivations for each individual and references all three texts. However, the explanations are mostly labels and do not fully connect the evidence to the claim with reasoning. For example, it says Donna is passionate about volcanoes because of storms and classes, but does not explain how these experiences led to her motivation. For Margaret, it mentions encouragement from parents and breaking barriers, but does not connect how this encouragement led to her actions. For "Dare to be Creative," it mentions motivation by creating questions, but does not explain how this drives the author. The response is text-engaged and addresses motivations, but lacks clear reasoning connecting evidence to claim, which fits Score 2.
